title: Reflexive Verbs, Deepened
description: Master the reflexive pronouns (me, te, se, nos, os, se) and where they go, the daily-routine verbs (levantarse, ducharse, acostarse), the meaning shifts (dormir vs dormirse, ir vs irse, poner vs ponerse), the reciprocal 'each other' (nos vemos, se quieren), reflexives for emotions, and reflexives in the past.

cards.front: me, te, se, nos, os, se
cards.notes: Reflexive verbs pair each subject with a pronoun: *yo* → **me**, *tú* → **te**, *él/ella/usted* → **se**, *nosotros* → **nos**, *vosotros* → **os**, *ellos/ustedes* → **se**. English usually omits 'myself' (*I wash*, not *I wash myself*), so the pronoun is easy to forget — but in Spanish it is required.

Before a conjugated verb, the pronoun goes **in front**: *Me levanto*, *Te levantas*, *Se levanta*. The infinitive *levantarse* keeps **-se** on the end; once conjugated, the **-se** becomes the matching pronoun.

cards.back: to get up, shower, get dressed, go to bed, wake up
cards.notes: The core daily-routine reflexives. Some also stem-change: *despertarse* → *me despierto* (e→ie), *acostarse* → *me acuesto* (o→ue), *vestirse* → *me visto* (e→i). The reflexive pronoun is separate from the stem change.

cards.front: Me voy a duchar. / Voy a ducharme.
cards.back: two correct positions with an infinitive
cards.notes: With *ir a + infinitive* (or any verb + infinitive), the pronoun can either go **before** the conjugated verb (*Me voy a duchar*) or **attach to the infinitive** (*Voy a ducharme*). Both are correct and mean the same. You may not split it: NOT *Voy me a duchar*.

cards.back: Get up! / Wake up! (pronoun attached to a command)
cards.notes: In affirmative commands the pronoun **attaches to the end**: *levanta* + *te* → **levántate** (note the added accent). Compare *Siéntate* (Sit down), *Acuéstate* (Go to bed). With a negative command it goes in front: *No te levantes*.

cards.front: dormir = to sleep / dormirse = to fall asleep
cards.notes: Adding the reflexive often shifts the meaning. *Dormir* is the ongoing state of sleeping; *dormirse* is the moment of falling asleep: *Dormí ocho horas* (I slept eight hours) vs *Me dormí en el sofá* (I fell asleep on the sofa).

cards.front: ir = to go / irse = to leave (go away)
cards.back: ir needs a destination; irse means to depart
cards.notes: *Ir* points to a destination (*Voy al cine* = I'm going to the cinema). *Irse* means to leave / go away from where you are (*Me voy* = I'm leaving / off I go). *¡Vámonos!* = Let's get going!

cards.front: poner = to put / ponerse = to put on; to become
cards.back: ponerse = to put on (clothes) or to become (emotion)
cards.notes: *Poner* = to put/place (*Pongo el libro en la mesa*). *Ponerse* = to put on clothes (*Me pongo el abrigo* = I put on my coat) OR to become (an emotion/state): *Se pone nervioso* = He gets nervous.

cards.front: Nos vemos mañana. / Se quieren mucho.
cards.notes: With a plural subject, the reflexive pronoun can mean **'each other'**: *Nos vemos* = We see each other / See you; *Se quieren* = They love each other; *Se escriben* = They write to each other. English needs 'each other'; Spanish just uses *nos/os/se*.

cards.back: to get nervous, get angry, become happy, get annoyed
cards.notes: Many change-of-state and emotion verbs are reflexive: *me enojo* (I get angry), *me alegro* (I'm glad), *me pongo nervioso* (I get nervous), *me aburro* (I get bored). Where English says 'get/become + adjective', Spanish often uses one reflexive verb.

cards.front: Me levanté temprano. / Se acostaron tarde.
cards.notes: In the past the pronoun still comes first; only the verb takes preterite endings: *Me levanté* (I got up), *Te duchaste*, *Se acostó*, *Nos despertamos*, *Se durmieron*. Don't drop the pronoun: NOT *Levanté temprano*.

Because English rarely marks reflexives ('I shower', not 'I shower myself'), learners often leave out the pronoun. In Spanish, *Ducho* alone means 'I shower (someone else)'. You must say **Me ducho** for 'I shower (myself)'.

steps.title: The reflexive pronouns
steps.body:
# Reflexive verbs, deepened

A **reflexive verb** describes an action the subject does to itself. Its infinitive ends in **-se** (*levantarse* = to get up). To use it, you pick the pronoun that matches the subject:

| Subject | Pronoun | Example |
|---|---|---|
| yo | **me** | me levanto |
| tú | **te** | te levantas |
| él/ella/usted | **se** | se levanta |
| nosotros | **nos** | nos levantamos |
| vosotros | **os** | os levantáis |
| ellos/ustedes | **se** | se levantan |

The verb itself conjugates normally; the pronoun just sits in front.

> ⚠️ **The #1 English-speaker trap.** English almost never says 'myself' (*I wash*, not *I wash myself*), so learners drop the pronoun. But *Ducho* means 'I shower **someone else**'. For 'I take a shower' you must say **Me ducho**. The pronoun is not optional.

steps.title: Where the pronoun goes
steps.body:
## Three positions

**1. Before a conjugated verb** (the default):

> **Me** levanto. / **Te** duchas. / **Se** acuesta.

**2. With an infinitive or *ir a + infinitive*** — two correct options:

> **Me** voy a duchar. = Voy a duchar**me**.

Either put the pronoun before the helper, or glue it to the infinitive. What you may **not** do is split it: NOT *Voy me a duchar*.

**3. Attached to an affirmative command:**

> ¡Levánta**te**! (Get up!)  ·  Siénta**te**. (Sit down.)

Note the written accent that appears when the pronoun is attached. With a **negative** command it goes back in front: *No **te** levantes.*

Daily-routine verbs to practice: *levantarse, ducharse, vestirse, acostarse, despertarse* (several also stem-change: *me despierto*, *me acuesto*, *me visto*).

steps.title: Meaning shifts and 'each other'
steps.body:
## The reflexive can change the meaning

Adding the pronoun often gives a verb a new sense:

| Plain | Reflexive |
|---|---|
| **dormir** = to sleep | **dormirse** = to fall asleep |
| **ir** = to go (somewhere) | **irse** = to leave / go away |
| **poner** = to put | **ponerse** = to put on; to become |

- *Dormí ocho horas* (I slept 8h) vs *Me dormí en clase* (I fell asleep).
- *Voy a casa* (I'm going home) vs *Me voy* (I'm leaving).
- *Pongo la taza aquí* (I put the cup here) vs *Me pongo el abrigo* (I put on my coat) vs *Se pone nervioso* (He gets nervous).

## Reciprocal: 'each other'

With a plural subject, *nos / os / se* can mean **each other**:

> **Nos** vemos mañana. = See you (We see each other) tomorrow.
> Ellos **se** quieren mucho. = They love each other a lot.

## Emotions and change of state

Many feelings are reflexive in Spanish: *enojarse* (get angry), *alegrarse* (be glad), *ponerse nervioso* (get nervous), *aburrirse* (get bored). And they work in the **past** too: *Me levanté temprano* (I got up early) — keep the pronoun!

exercise.prompt: Match each subject with its reflexive pronoun.

accept: Yo me levanto a las siete.
exercise.hint: yo → me. The pronoun goes before the verb: Me levanto.

exercise.prompt: Match each reflexive verb with its English meaning.
Match each verb with its meaning. Watch how the reflexive -se changes things!
Fill in the reflexive pronoun for *tú*: 'You shower in the morning.'
exercise.hint: tú → te. The pronoun goes right before the conjugated verb.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *I'm going to shower.* (attach the pronoun to the infinitive)
exercise.hint: With ir a + infinitive you may attach the pronoun: ducharme.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the affirmative command 'Get up!' (levantarse, tú). Attach the pronoun.
exercise.hint: levanta + te = levántate (with an accent on the á).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I fell asleep on the sofa.* (use dormirse, past)
accept: Yo me dormí en el sofá.
exercise.hint: 'fall asleep' = dormirse → me dormí (preterite, with the pronoun).
'get/become' here = ponerse → se pone; nervous (she) = nerviosa.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *We see each other tomorrow.* (verse, reciprocal)
exercise.hint: 'each other' with nosotros = nos. nos + vemos = Nos vemos.

exercise.prompt: Which sentence correctly means 'I take a shower (myself)'?
exercise.hint: yo → me. Without the pronoun, 'Ducho' means you shower someone else.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the missing pronoun: 'I got up early yesterday.'
exercise.hint: Even in the past, keep the pronoun: yo → me levanté.
'to leave / go away' = irse → se fueron (preterite of ir + se).
